title: How Recent Policy And Tariff Shifts Are Reframing First Solar's Story And Valuation
text: First Solar's narrative has shifted dramatically in response to recent policy and tariff changes. The company's domestic manufacturing footprint, once viewed as a competitive disadvantage due to higher costs, now represents a significant strategic advantage. Trump administration tariffs on imported solar modules have created a favorable environment for First Solar's U.S. production facilities. The company's projected tariff impact in 2026 is estimated at $155 million–$175 million gross and $125 million–$135 million net, pressuring profitability but manageable within strategic context. First Solar issued a conservative financial outlook for 2026, forecasting revenue between $4.9 billion and $5.2 billion—approximately flat versus 2025—but this conservative stance reflects strategic selectivity rather than weakness. The company cited a freeze on approvals for major projects under the current administration, creating a direct bottleneck as new solar projects cannot move forward. The company's ability to monetize Section 45X tax credits—projected at $2.10-$2.19 billion for 2026—provides substantial earnings support that may not be fully appreciated by the market. With 47.9 GW of contracted backlog valued at $14.4 billion through 2030, First Solar has substantial visibility. The company's positioning to benefit from U.S. manufacturing incentives while managing tariff headwinds represents a complex valuation scenario that different investor bases assess differently.
